{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.3,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.3",
    "R1.4",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "When you say “Make this stronger,” which scope should we take? (1) Language polish only (clarity, concision, assertive tone; no claim or structure changes), (2) Substantive reframing of the thesis/novelty and alignment of claims to the reported evidence (may tighten or temper “first” and “deployment-ready”), or (3) both 1 and 2?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Strengthening likely involves sharpening the core thesis and contribution framing so the central claim is crisper and more compelling.",
    "R1.2": "Ensuring the argument from motivation to system design to results is logically tight is essential to perceived strength.",
    "R1.3": "Claims such as cost savings, latency bounds, and policy robustness should be clearly tied to the reported measurements.",
    "R1.4": "Bold 'first' claims and positioning benefit from calibrated novelty statements relative to plausible prior approaches.",
    "R1.6": "Tone, grammar, and concision are key to making the prose feel stronger without altering content.",
    "RX.1": "Any strengthening should transparently note limits (e.g., 12-node scale, specific workloads) to avoid overstated certainty.",
    "RX.2": "Stronger writing must avoid unqualified overclaims, especially around 'first' and 'deployment-ready' assertions.",
    "RX.3": "Work should remain within the provided artifact’s scope and not introduce new experiments or external facts.",
    "RX.4": "Edits should avoid encouraging premature production deployment or other risky interpretations of the results.",
    "RX.5": "Revised emphases should remain traceable to the text’s stated methods, setups, and quantitative results."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: When you say “Make this stronger,” which scope should we take? (1) Language polish only (clarity, concision, assertive tone; no claim or structure changes), (2) Substantive reframing of the thesis/novelty and alignment of claims to the reported evidence (may tighten or temper “first” and “deployment-ready”), or (3) both 1 and 2?

rationale.R1.1: Strengthening likely involves sharpening the core thesis and contribution framing so the central claim is crisper and more compelling.
rationale.R1.2: Ensuring the argument from motivation to system design to results is logically tight is essential to perceived strength.
rationale.R1.3: Claims such as cost savings, latency bounds, and policy robustness should be clearly tied to the reported measurements.
rationale.R1.4: Bold 'first' claims and positioning benefit from calibrated novelty statements relative to plausible prior approaches.
rationale.R1.6: Tone, grammar, and concision are key to making the prose feel stronger without altering content.
rationale.RX.1: Any strengthening should transparently note limits (e.g., 12-node scale, specific workloads) to avoid overstated certainty.
rationale.RX.2: Stronger writing must avoid unqualified overclaims, especially around 'first' and 'deployment-ready' assertions.
rationale.RX.3: Work should remain within the provided artifact’s scope and not introduce new experiments or external facts.
rationale.RX.4: Edits should avoid encouraging premature production deployment or other risky interpretations of the results.
rationale.RX.5: Revised emphases should remain traceable to the text’s stated methods, setups, and quantitative results.
